queue.title: Sales Is Not What You Think It Is
queue.hook: Most founders think sales is about talking. It is not.
queue.script:
Sales is not talking. It's about listening. It is finding out what someone is afraid of, what their problem is and showing them you're the solution.

Here is what I've learned;

One. The person who talks most in a sales call usually loses.

Two. Every objection is just a question they do not know how to ask.

Three. People don't buy features.  They buy the version of themselves where the problem doesn't exist.

Four. Follow up is where most deals are actually won. Not the pitch.

Learn to sell or learn to stay broke. There is opportunity everywhere.

queue.title: Nobody Is Coming To Save Your Business
queue.hook: No investor. No partnership. No viral post.
queue.script:
No investor. No partnership. No viral post.

Nobody is coming to save your business.

The people who are doing well right now are not waiting for a break. They went out and made the calls. They had the awkward conversations. They sold before the product was perfect.

You are sitting on an idea that could actually work. But ideas without action are just thoughts.

The world is not waiting for you. It is moving past you while you are still deciding.

There is opportunity everywhere.  This is your sign to take action.

queue.title: Opportunity Is Hiding In Plain Sight
queue.hook: The biggest opportunity of your life might be something you dismiss every single day.
queue.script:
Start paying attention to what people complain about. 

The biggest opportunity of your life might be something you dismiss every single day.

Every industry has something that everyone in it complains about but nobody has fixed. That complaint is a product waiting to be built.

Every workflow has something people do manually that you could build a demo of in an afternoon. That friction is a business.  

You do not need a completely new idea. You need to pay attention to what people are suffering through right in front of you.  If they are suffering through it, you can pretty much guarantee others are too.

Opportunity is not hiding, it's everywhere.

queue.title: Validation Is Overrated
queue.hook: Everyone tells you to validate before building. I think that advice is half right.
queue.script:
Validate before building. That advice is half right.

You need to validate the problem. Not the solution.  Be 100% sure there's a problem before you explore what a solution could look like. 

Most founders go validate whether people LIKE their specific product. That is the wrong question.

The right question is: how much does this problem cost you right now? How are you solving it today? What would you pay for it to be gone?

Get obsessed with the problem. Stay loose on the solution. Your first version will be wrong anyway.  

The market does not care about you or your product. It cares about its own pain.

queue.title: You Have Not Run Out Of Opportunities
queue.hook: You have not run out of opportunities. You have run out of courage.
queue.script:
You have not run out of opportunities. You have run out of courage.  You've been beaten down by repeated failures. 

But the ideas are there. The market is there. The tools have never been cheaper or more accessible.

But you need to keep going.  

What is missing is the willingness to be seen trying something and potentially failing publicly.

Every day you wait, someone with half your insight and twice your courage is building the thing you thought of.

queue.title: How To Validate An Idea In 48 Hours
queue.hook: You do not need months to know if an idea works. You need 48 hours and a direct message.
queue.script:
Don't waste 6 months of you life obsessing over something nobody wants.  You do not need months to know if an idea works. You need 48 hours and a direct message.

Here is the framework:

Day one. Write a one-sentence description of the problem you solve. Message 20 people who have that problem. Ask if they have it. Do not mention your solution yet.

Day two. For everyone who confirmed the problem, tell them what you are building. Ask if they would pay for it. Ask what they are doing right now instead.

If you get three people willing to pay before you build anything, you have something.

If nobody engages, the problem either does not exist or you have the wrong customer.

Fort-eight hours. That is all you need to not waste six months of your life.

queue.title: The Curiosity Content Play
queue.hook: What if the most powerful content you could post is something you know almost nothing about yet?
queue.script:
Some of the most powerful content you can post is something you know almost nothing about.

Curiosity content. Documenting the learning. Not the expert explaining. Be the beginner exploring.

People do not just follow expertise. They follow authenticity. They follow the journey. The mistakes and corrections in real time.

Start something you know nothing about but are interested in. Post about it from day one. The audience you build around that journey could be larger than the one you built around your expertise.

Because everyone was once a beginner. But people are embarrassed to fail or be seen as incompetent.  So very few people document it.  

Like Austin Butler said, "Embarrassment is an underexplored emotion, go out there and make a fool of yourself"

queue.title: 3 Sales Mistakes Founders Make Every Day
queue.hook: Three sales mistakes I see founders make every single day.
queue.script:
Three sales mistakes I see founders make every single day.

One. Leading with the product instead of the problem. Nobody cares what you built. They care what problem it solves. Start there.

Two. Selling to everyone. If your product is for everyone, it is for no one. The narrower you go in the beginning, the faster you find product-market fit.

Three. Giving up after one no. Most sales happen after the fifth follow-up. Most founders follow up once. Sometimes twice. Then they assume it is a no.

Fix these three things and you'll see a uplift in your conversion rate.

queue.title: Stop Waiting For Permission
queue.hook: Nobody is going to give you permission to start your business.
queue.script:
Nobody is going to give you permission to start your business.

Not your family. Not your current employer. Not the market.

You are waiting for a signal that is never coming.

The successful founders did not wait until they were ready. They started when it was uncomfortable, when they did not have all the answers, when the timing was not perfect.

The timing is never perfect.

Start anyway.

queue.title: Your Market Research Is Worthless
queue.hook: Your market research is worthless if you have not spoken to a single customer.
queue.script:
Your market research is worthless if you have not spoken to a single customer.

Reports. TAM calculations. Competitor analysis. None of it tells you whether anyone will pay you for your specific solution.

I have seen founders with beautiful decks and terrible businesses.

And I have seen founders with no deck and 50 paying customers who figured it out through conversations.

Get off the slides. Get on the phone. Twenty conversations will teach you more than twenty research reports.

queue.title: You Are Sitting On A Business Right Now
queue.hook: There is a very good chance you are sitting on a viable business and do not know it.
queue.script:
There is a very good chance you are sitting on a viable business and do not know it.

Every skill you have developed. Every frustration you have experienced. Every process you have figured out that others have not. Every unfair advantage from your specific background and experience.

All of that is potential money making business.

Most people dismiss their own knowledge because they assume if they know it, everyone knows it.

What is obvious to you is a mystery to someone else. And someone will pay to shortcut that gap.

queue.title: The 3 Types Of Founder
queue.hook: In my experience, there are three types of founder. Only one of them builds a real business.
queue.script:
In my experience, there are three types of founder. If they are a solo founder, only one of them builds a real business.

The Thinker. Has great ideas. Analyzes extensively. Rarely ships. Keeps refining. Usually stays stuck in the planning phase forever.

The Builder. Ships constantly. Moves fast. But does not talk to customers enough. Builds things people do not need then wonders why it does not grow.

The Seller. Uncomfortable with uncertainty but stays in motion. Talks to customers constantly. Builds what they confirm they want. Ships imperfect things and improves from real feedback.

Most people start as thinkers or builders. The job is to become the seller.  You can be all 3, but at least 60% of you needs to be the Seller.   Thats the whole game.

bank.title: 5 Things That Kill Early Stage Startups
bank.hook: After talking to hundreds of early stage founders, the same five things kill them every time.
bank.script:
After talking to hundreds of early stage founders, the same five things kill them every time.

One. Building before validating. You spent six months building something nobody confirmed they would pay for.

Two. Waiting too long to sell. Sales conversations start finding out if something works.

Three. Solving the problem they described instead of the problem they have. These are different.

Four. Hiring too early. Four people burning through runway on a product that has not found its market yet.

Five. Giving up at month four when the results were going to come at month eight.

None of these are complicated. All of them are avoidable.
